Patient_History.Chief_Complaint: Severe chest pain radiating to the back
Patient_History.History_of_Present_Illness: The patient experienced sudden onset of severe chest pain while at rest, described as tearing and radiating to the back. Pain intensity is 9/10, not relieved by rest or position change.
Patient_History.Family_History: Father deceased from myocardial infarction at age 60
Patient_History.Social_History: Non-smoker, occasional alcohol use, no illicit drug use

Physical_Examination.Cardiovascular: Irregular pulse, diminished peripheral pulses in lower extremities

Paraclinical_Investigations.Echocardiography: Normal ejection fraction; no pericardial effusion

Pathophysiology_and_Etiology.Description: Aortic dissection occurs due to a tear in the intimal layer of the aorta, creating a false lumen. This can lead to catastrophic complications including rupture and compromise of blood supply to vital organs. Hypertension is a major risk factor due to increased shear stress on the aortic wall.
Pathophysiology_and_Etiology.Etiology: The patient's poorly controlled hypertension is a significant contributing factor. Genetic predisposition and hyperlipidemia may also play roles.

Management.Initial_Management: Immediate blood pressure control with intravenous beta-blockers (e.g., Esmolol) to reduce aortic wall stress.
Management.Definitive_Management: Emergency surgical intervention with aortic repair or replacement.
Management.Monitoring: Continuous hemodynamic monitoring in an intensive care setting is critical pre- and post-operatively.

Disposition.Inpatient_Care: Admitted to the cardiothoracic intensive care unit.
Disposition.Follow_Up: Regular follow-up with cardiology and cardiovascular surgery teams post-discharge.

Evidence_and_References.Guidelines: European Society of Cardiology (ESC) guidelines on the management of aortic diseases.
Evidence_and_References.Studies: IRAD (International Registry of Acute Aortic Dissection) findings on outcomes and management strategies.

Teaching_and_Reasoning_Panel.Discussion: The sudden onset and nature of the pain, along with the patient's history of hypertension, guide the diagnosis towards aortic dissection. Imaging confirming the dissection solidifies the diagnosis. The case underscores the importance of rapid intervention in Type A dissections to prevent mortality.

Expert_Panel_Consensus.Consensus: All experts agree on the diagnosis and management strategy, emphasizing the urgency of surgical intervention in Type A dissections.

Conclusion.Summary: This case highlights the critical nature of diagnosing and managing aortic dissection, particularly Type A, where timely surgical intervention is life-saving.

Atypical_Presentations.Variations: Atypical presentations can include neurological symptoms due to compromised cerebral blood flow or abdominal pain if visceral arteries are involved. These require a high index of suspicion and prompt imaging.
